title: Paper clay mobile of fish shapes
subtitle: A hanging mobile of five lightweight paper clay fish suspended on cord
excerpt: A hanging mobile of five small fish shapes cut from rolled paper clay, textured and pierced, and hung on cord from a wooden cross-bar. Paper clay is the right choice for a mobile because the finished pieces are lighter than plain air-dry clay at the same thickness, and the cellulose fibres reinforce the thin fins and tails. No kiln required. Decorative indoor hanging.
sourceNotes: Paper clay slab construction and small hanging-form work derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for fish-form ceramic reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay with cellulose fibres mixed in, which strengthens joins and thin sections and reduces cracking. Lighter than plain clay at the same thickness, useful for mobile pieces.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For paper clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A small hanging mobile of five fish shapes cut from rolled
paper clay

. Each fish is roughly 80 mm long, cut from a 4 mm slab, with a single hole pierced near the dorsal edge for the suspension cord. Paper clay is the right material here because it produces a lighter finished piece than plain air-dry clay at the same thickness, and the cellulose fibres reinforce the thin tail fins. No kiln required. The mobile hangs indoors as a decorative piece.

items.name: 4 mm guide rails
items.substitution: Two stacks of two wooden ice-lolly sticks

items.name: Card fish template (80 mm long)
items.substitution: Cut around a drawn shape on stiff card

items.name: Drinking straw or piping nozzle (3 mm)
Wooden dowel (300 mm long, 10 mm diameter)

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the paper clay for two minutes on the board. Paper clay needs gentler wedging than plain clay; over-wedging breaks the cellulose fibres. Cut the card template: a fish outline, 80 mm long from snout to tail tip, 35 mm tall at the body. Draw the outline on stiff card and cut around the line with sharp scissors.
Cutting the fish
1. Roll the slab

Pat the clay into a thick rectangle and lay the guide rails on either side. Roll between the rails for an even 4 mm thickness. A thinner slab here keeps the finished mobile light enough to turn freely in a draught. The slab should measure about 22 cm by 16 cm to give space for five fish with cutting margins.
2. Press optional texture

Press a stamp, a piece of lace, or a textured fabric across the slab for a scale-like pattern. Roll lightly across the texture piece with the rolling pin for an even impression, then lift the piece straight up. Skip this step if the fish are to be painted with scale detail later instead.
3. Cut the five fish

Lay the card template on the slab and cut around it with the fettling knife held vertical, keeping the blade slow and steady. Move the template along the slab and cut another. Cut five fish in total, alternating direction (snout facing left, then right) so the finished mobile reads as a school in motion.
4. Refine the cut edges

Run the wooden rib along each cut edge to compress and smooth. Press lightly so the texture on the face is not flattened. A slightly bevelled upper edge is better than a knife-sharp corner: it softens the silhouette and is less prone to chipping during drying.
5. Add eyes and gill detail

Press a small indent for an eye near the snout of each fish with the back end of a pencil or a small modelling tool. A single short curved score with the needle tool behind the eye suggests the gill. Add a few short parallel scores along the tail for fin detail. Keep the marks light; they read more strongly after drying than they look at the wet stage.
6. Pierce the hanging hole

Press the 3 mm straw straight through each fish 8 mm down from the dorsal edge, roughly above the eye so the fish hangs level (head not higher than tail). Twist the straw gently as it comes back out. Always pierce while the clay is still soft.
Drying

Set the fish flat on the board. Flat pieces curl as they dry if one face dries faster than the other. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 8 hours, then flip each fish over and uncover. Place a clean tile or piece of card on top of the fish for the first hour after flipping to weight them flat. The fish reach
leather-hard
in about 8 to 12 hours and
bone-dry
over a further one to two days.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand each fish lightly with 220-grit sandpaper, paying particular attention to the cut edges and around the hanging holes. Brush off the dust. Paint with acrylics: a single colour wash catches the texture; or paint each fish a different colour for a more playful set. Seal both faces with two coats of acrylic varnish. The paper clay is lighter than plain clay but still benefits from sealing to keep dust off the surface.
Assembling the mobile

Cut five lengths of cord, varying from 15 cm to 35 cm. Thread one cord through the hanging hole of each fish and tie a knot at the fish end so it cannot pull through. Tie the other end of each cord onto the wooden dowel at evenly spaced points along its length. Cut a single longer length of cord (about 80 cm), tie one end to each end of the dowel, and bring the centre of this length up as a single hanging loop.

Hang the loop temporarily from a hook and check the dowel hangs level. Adjust the hanging-loop position along the cord if one side is heavier than the other; a small bead or a knot tied along the cord acts as a slide-adjustable balance point.
Care

Paper clay is not waterproof. Hang indoors, away from direct sunlight (which fades acrylic colours over time) and away from kitchen steam. Dust occasionally with a soft cloth. The mobile turns gently in any small draught; hang somewhere a window or doorway provides natural movement.
Variations
Use leaf, bird, or moon shapes instead of fish; the cutting-and-piercing method is the same regardless of silhouette.
Cut nine fish for a longer mobile, hung on two parallel dowels at right angles for a three-dimensional cluster.
Cut larger fish (120 mm long) for a more substantial single-bar mobile; the same paper clay handles this scale without warping.

items.symptom: Fish curl up at the tail as they dry
items.cause: One face dried faster than the other
items.fix: Flip each fish at 8 hours and weight flat with a tile for the first hour after flipping; cover loosely with plastic for the initial drying

items.symptom: Tail fins crack at the tip
items.cause: Drying ran too fast at the thin section, or plain air-dry clay was used
items.fix: Always use paper clay for thin sections; cover the fish with plastic during the first 8 hours; press fresh paper clay slurry into any crack before sealing

items.symptom: Hanging hole tears when the fish is hung
items.cause: Hole pierced too close to the edge
items.fix: Pierce at least 8 mm down from the dorsal edge; for a fish already torn, thread the cord through a small loop of card glued to the back as a reinforcement

items.cause: Cord positions on the dowel are uneven, or the hanging loop is not centred over the dowel's balance point
items.fix: Slide the hanging loop along the cord until the dowel sits level. A sliding knot lets you shift the point without re-tying
